subtopic_detail: Kawasaki disease recognition and IVIG window
stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a 3-year-old non-Aboriginal boy on day 6 of fever. He has bilateral non-purulent conjunctival injection, cracked red lips with a strawberry tongue, a polymorphous truncal rash, and a single left cervical lymph node measuring 17 mm. His hands and feet are swollen and erythematous. He is irritable but not septic. His mother is asking when his fever will resolve.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.1 °C, HR 158, RR 32, SpO2 98% room air, BP 95/55 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Bulbar conjunctival injection without exudate, fissured lips, indurated palms and soles, polymorphous trunk rash, one left cervical node 17 mm, no meningism, chest clear, no murmur, soft abdomen
data_table.Investigations: WCC 17.5 x10^9/L (neutrophils 13.2), platelets 555 x10^9/L, CRP 145 mg/L, ESR 78 mm/h, ALT 92 U/L, albumin 28 g/L, urinalysis 25 WBC/HPF sterile, throat swab negative for group A strep

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence intravenous flucloxacillin and ceftriaxone for presumed bacterial cervical lymphadenitis with systemic features and review cultures at 48 hours
options.rationale: A solitary 17 mm node with multisystem mucocutaneous findings and sterile pyuria does not fit bacterial adenitis; antibiotic delay risks coronary aneurysm formation. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Admit, commence intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg over 12 hours and oral aspirin, and arrange a baseline transthoracic echocardiogram
options.rationale: Five days of fever plus four of five principal criteria meet the diagnostic threshold; IVIG given before day 10 of illness reduces coronary artery aneurysm risk from roughly 25% to under 5%. Baseline echocardiography establishes a comparator for coronary surveillance. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Send paired viral PCR including adenovirus and measles, withhold treatment, and review in 48 hours once the viral panel results have been formally reported
options.rationale: A 48-hour observation period beyond day 6 of fever wastes the IVIG efficacy window; viral mimics rarely produce this combination of mucosal, extremity and laboratory findings. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Commence high-dose oral prednisolone 2 mg/kg daily as initial monotherapy and review in the general paediatric clinic within three days
options.rationale: Australian first-line treatment is IVIG plus aspirin; corticosteroid monotherapy is not standard initial therapy and is reserved for IVIG-resistant or high-risk subgroups. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Reassure that this is viral exanthem with reactive adenopathy, prescribe regular paracetamol and oral fluids, and review in primary care in 48 hours if not improving
options.rationale: Discharge would miss the IVIG window; the constellation of findings beyond five days of fever meets clinical Kawasaki disease criteria and mandates inpatient treatment. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

explanation.summary: Kawasaki disease is diagnosed clinically when fever lasts five or more days with at least four of five principal features (conjunctivitis, oral changes, polymorphous rash, extremity changes, cervical lymphadenopathy over 15 mm). Untreated, coronary artery aneurysms develop in around 25% of children. IVIG 2 g/kg with aspirin given before day 10 reduces this to under 5%. Baseline echocardiography and follow-up at 2 and 6 weeks track coronary involvement.
key_points: Diagnosis requires fever 5 or more days plus 4 of 5 principal features in classic Kawasaki disease
IVIG 2 g/kg with aspirin should be given before day 10 of illness
Coronary aneurysm risk falls from around 25% to under 5% with timely IVIG
Baseline echocardiogram is performed at diagnosis with follow-up at 2 and 6 weeks
Incomplete Kawasaki disease may be treated on fewer criteria if inflammatory markers and supportive features are present
explanation.pearls: Children under 12 months often present with incomplete Kawasaki disease and have the highest coronary aneurysm risk; CRP over 30 mg/L plus albumin under 30 g/L lowers the diagnostic threshold to 2 of 5 criteria.

stem: You are the special care nursery resident reviewing a 60-hour-old term non-Aboriginal infant born at 39+2 weeks by uncomplicated vaginal birth. The midwives noted yellow sclerae and the transcutaneous bilirubinometer reads above the screening threshold. The mother is blood group O Rh-positive; cord blood was not sent. The baby is breastfeeding 6 times in 24 hours and has lost 7% of birth weight. There is no cephalohaematoma and no family history of haemolytic disease.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9 °C, HR 144, RR 46, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Alert, jaundice to mid-thigh, no plethora, no hepatosplenomegaly, normal tone and Moro, suck coordinated
data_table.Investigations: Serum bilirubin 320 micromol/L (gestation-and-age threshold for phototherapy 290; exchange 430), conjugated fraction 6 micromol/L, FBC Hb 175 g/L reticulocytes 4%, blood group O Rh-positive, direct antiglobulin test negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Prepare for double-volume exchange transfusion under intensive care given the rising bilirubin trajectory and risk of acute bilirubin encephalopathy
options.rationale: Exchange transfusion is reserved for bilirubin at or above the exchange threshold or for signs of acute bilirubin encephalopathy; this baby is well below exchange. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Jaundice)

options.text: Withhold phototherapy, supplement with formula top-ups of 30 mL every three hours, and repeat the bilirubin in 12 hours before deciding treatment
options.rationale: Routine formula top-ups are not indicated when feeding is adequate and weight loss is under 10%; this delay risks crossing the exchange threshold. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Jaundice)

options.text: Commence intensive phototherapy, continue frequent breastfeeding with lactation support, and repeat serum bilirubin in 6 hours to confirm a downward trend
options.rationale: Serum bilirubin sits above the phototherapy threshold for this gestation and age; intensive phototherapy with ongoing breastfeeding is first-line and a 6-hour repeat captures rebound or treatment failure. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Jaundice)

options.text: Commence intravenous immunoglobulin infusion to slow ongoing haemolysis and arrange transfer to the neonatal intensive care nursery for cardiorespiratory monitoring
options.rationale: IVIG is used for proven isoimmune haemolytic disease with a rapidly rising bilirubin; the direct antiglobulin test is negative and there is no evidence of haemolysis. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Jaundice)

options.text: Discharge home with parental education on jaundice warning signs and arrange routine community midwife review at 5 days of life for a repeat bilirubin
options.rationale: Discharge with a bilirubin above the phototherapy threshold risks bilirubin neurotoxicity; treatment is required before community follow-up. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Jaundice)

explanation.summary: Australian neonatal jaundice management uses gestation-and-age-specific phototherapy and exchange thresholds plotted against postnatal hours. Term infants with unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia above the phototherapy line need intensive phototherapy and continued breastfeeding. A repeat bilirubin at 4 to 6 hours assesses response and excludes ongoing rise toward exchange. Conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia above 20% or 20 micromol/L always prompts a separate cholestasis workup.
key_points: Phototherapy and exchange thresholds vary by gestation and hours of age in Australian nomograms
Intensive phototherapy plus continued breastfeeding is first-line above the phototherapy line
Repeat serum bilirubin at 4 to 6 hours to confirm downward trend
Negative direct antiglobulin test plus normal haemoglobin and reticulocytes argues against isoimmune haemolysis
Conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia is always pathological and prompts cholestasis workup
explanation.pearls: A conjugated bilirubin over 20 micromol/L or over 20% of total in any jaundiced infant at any age mandates same-day investigation for biliary atresia because Kasai portoenterostomy outcomes deteriorate sharply after 60 days of life.

subtopic_detail: Paediatric anaphylaxis intramuscular adrenaline dose band
stem: You are the emergency department resident called to a 4-year-old non-Aboriginal boy who collapsed at a family meal where he was unknowingly given cashew. Within 10 minutes he developed urticaria, lip swelling and audible wheeze. He has known anaphylaxis to peanut. His EpiPen Jr was administered by his father 3 minutes before arrival. On arrival he remains wheezy with a hoarse cry and is sleepy but rousable.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 °C, HR 156, RR 36, SpO2 90% room air rising to 96% on 8 L/min via face mask, BP 78/40 mmHg, weight estimated 16 kg by Broselow tape
data_table.Examination: Generalised urticaria, marked lip and tongue swelling, audible stridor and biphasic wheeze, capillary refill 4 seconds, GCS 13 (eyes 3, verbal 4, motor 6)
data_table.Investigations: Bedside glucose 4.6 mmol/L; no further investigations yet performed

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Administer intramuscular promethazine 0.5 mg/kg, give nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1:1000, and arrange urgent ENT review for the stridor
options.rationale: Antihistamines do not treat airway or circulatory features; nebulised adrenaline is adjunctive for upper airway oedema only and never replaces IM adrenaline. (Source: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis)

options.text: Administer intramuscular adrenaline 1:1000 at 0.30 mL into the lateral thigh, give 200 mg hydrocortisone IV, and continue 8 L oxygen via face mask
options.rationale: 0.30 mL of 1:1000 is the adult dose, twice the band for a 16 kg child; hydrocortisone has no role in acute resuscitation and delays definitive measures. (Source: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis)

options.text: Administer intravenous adrenaline 10 micrograms/kg bolus once intravenous access is established, sit him upright and prepare nebulised salbutamol
options.rationale: IV adrenaline bolus risks severe hypertension and arrhythmia in an unmonitored child; an IV infusion at 0.1 to 1 micrograms/kg/min is the correct escalation, not a bolus. Upright posture worsens distributive shock. (Source: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis)

options.text: Administer intramuscular adrenaline 1:1000 at 0.15 mL into the lateral thigh, lay him flat with legs elevated, and prepare a 20 mL/kg crystalloid bolus
options.rationale: Persistent anaphylaxis after one EpiPen Jr requires a repeat dose; the paediatric weight-band for under 20 kg is 0.15 mg (0.15 mL of 1:1000) IM. Supine positioning with legs elevated and crystalloid expansion treats distributive shock. (Source: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis)

options.text: Withhold further adrenaline as one dose has been given, administer nebulised salbutamol 5 mg back-to-back, and reassess after the bronchodilator has been delivered
options.rationale: Anaphylaxis with persistent hypotension and airway features mandates repeat IM adrenaline every 5 minutes until resolved; withholding risks death from distributive shock or airway obstruction. (Source: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis)

explanation.summary: Anaphylaxis is treated with prompt intramuscular adrenaline 1:1000 dosed by weight band, with supine posture for hypotension and crystalloid resuscitation. ASCIA dosing is 0.15 mg (0.15 mL) for 7.5 to 20 kg, 0.3 mg (0.3 mL) for over 20 kg, repeating every 5 minutes if symptoms persist. Antihistamines and corticosteroids do not save the airway or restore blood pressure and must not delay adrenaline. After two doses persistent shock requires an adrenaline infusion in a monitored setting.
key_points: IM adrenaline 1:1000 lateral thigh is first-line by weight band: 0.15 mg under 20 kg, 0.3 mg over 20 kg
Repeat every 5 minutes if symptoms persist or recur
Lay the patient flat with legs elevated for hypotension; never sit upright with shock
Crystalloid 20 mL/kg bolus for hypotension that does not respond to first adrenaline
Antihistamines and corticosteroids are adjuncts only and never replace adrenaline
explanation.pearls: Two or more IM adrenaline doses or any persistent hypotension is the threshold to start an adrenaline infusion at 0.1 to 1 micrograms/kg/min and call retrieval if the child is in a non-tertiary centre.

stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a 6-week-old non-Aboriginal infant with a 9-day history of coryza now followed by 3 days of paroxysmal coughing spells ending in cyanosis and post-tussive vomiting. There is no inspiratory whoop. He has had one apnoeic episode of 25 seconds witnessed by his mother. His older brother had a bronchitic illness 3 weeks ago. The mother declined pertussis vaccination in pregnancy; the infant has not yet received his 6-week immunisations. The household includes a 28-week pregnant aunt.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.4 °C, HR 168, RR 56 between coughs, SpO2 91% room air, 96% on 1 L nasal prongs
data_table.Examination: Alert between coughs, mild subcostal recession, chest clear between paroxysms, no focal crackles or wheeze, capillary refill 2 seconds
data_table.Investigations: WCC 32 x10^9/L with absolute lymphocytosis 22 x10^9/L, CRP 8 mg/L, chest X-ray clear, nasopharyngeal PCR pending for Bordetella pertussis

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge home with reassurance pending the nasopharyngeal PCR result, withhold antibiotics until the test confirms a positive result, and review in 48 hours in the general paediatric clinic
options.rationale: Treatment should be empiric for infants with clinically compatible pertussis; awaiting PCR delays therapy that reduces transmission and may worsen the apnoea risk. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Discharge home on oral amoxicillin 25 mg/kg three times daily for 7 days with parental education on cough monitoring and ambulance criteria for cyanotic episodes
options.rationale: Amoxicillin has no useful activity against Bordetella pertussis; an infant with witnessed apnoea cannot be safely managed at home. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Admit, commence oral erythromycin ethyl succinate 40 mg/kg/day in 4 divided doses for 7 days, and continue intravenous fluid maintenance until feeding improves
options.rationale: Erythromycin in infants under one month carries a recognised pyloric stenosis risk and is poorly tolerated; azithromycin is preferred in this age group in Australia. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Admit, commence intravenous benzylpenicillin 60 mg/kg every 6 hours, and arrange a lumbar puncture to exclude bacterial meningitis as a cause of the apnoea
options.rationale: Benzylpenicillin does not cover pertussis; the lymphocytosis, paroxysmal cough and post-tussive vomiting fit pertussis far better than meningitis and a lumbar puncture is not justified here. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Admit to a monitored bed, commence oral azithromycin 10 mg/kg daily for 5 days, and arrange household contact post-exposure prophylaxis through public health
options.rationale: Infants under 6 months with suspected pertussis and apnoea require admission for monitoring; azithromycin is first-line in this age group and reduces transmission. Household contacts including the pregnant aunt warrant prophylaxis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Pertussis in infants under 6 months presents with apnoea, paroxysmal cough and post-tussive vomiting, often without the classic whoop. Admission for cardiorespiratory monitoring is standard, and azithromycin is the preferred macrolide because erythromycin in neonates increases pyloric stenosis risk. Household contacts including pregnant women and other infants require post-exposure prophylaxis. Maternal pertussis vaccination at 20 to 32 weeks of every pregnancy is the most effective prevention.
key_points: Infants under 6 months with pertussis often present without the classic whoop
Apnoea, lymphocytosis and post-tussive vomiting are the key clues
Azithromycin 10 mg/kg daily for 5 days is the first-line antibiotic in young infants
Erythromycin in neonates is associated with infantile hypertrophic pyloric stenosis
Maternal pertussis vaccination at 20 to 32 weeks is the most effective infant prevention
explanation.pearls: A peripheral white cell count over 20 x10^9/L with marked lymphocytosis (over 50% lymphocytes) in an infant with paroxysmal cough has a high positive predictive value for pertussis and is associated with severe disease and pulmonary hypertension.

subtopic_detail: Reye-like syndrome in a child given aspirin during varicella
stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a 7-year-old non-Aboriginal girl admitted with 24 hours of incessant vomiting, irritability and increasing somnolence. Five days earlier she developed a vesicular rash that her grandmother managed with regular oral aspirin 300 mg every 4 hours obtained from an overseas pharmacy. She has no other medical conditions and her immunisations are otherwise up to date. There is no fever, no diarrhoea and no recent travel. Her mother brought her in because she became confused and combative an hour ago.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 °C, HR 132, RR 28 with intermittent yawning, SpO2 98% room air, BP 108/60 mmHg, capillary glucose 3.2 mmol/L
data_table.Examination: Crusted varicella lesions on trunk and scalp, GCS 11 (eyes 2, verbal 4, motor 5) with hyperreflexia and brisk plantars, no neck stiffness, no focal deficit, liver edge palpable 3 cm below costal margin
data_table.Investigations: ALT 1180 U/L, AST 1420 U/L, ammonia 165 micromol/L (normal under 50), INR 2.4, bilirubin 22 micromol/L, glucose 3.2 mmol/L, lactate 4.8 mmol/L, paracetamol level not detected, salicylate 1.8 mmol/L, CT brain shows diffuse cerebral oedema without focal lesion

lead_in: What is the single best initial intervention?

options.text: Administer N-acetylcysteine 150 mg/kg loading dose, withhold dextrose to avoid hyperglycaemia, and transfer to a ward bed for further serial liver function monitoring
options.rationale: N-acetylcysteine treats paracetamol toxicity and has unproven benefit here; withholding dextrose in hypoglycaemia worsens cerebral oedema and a ward bed is unsafe with GCS 11. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Liver Failure)

options.text: Cease aspirin, escalate to paediatric intensive care for intracranial pressure management with intravenous 10% dextrose, hypertonic saline and head-of-bed elevation
options.rationale: The clinical picture is Reye-like encephalopathy from salicylate exposure during varicella: hyperammonaemia, hypoglycaemia, transaminitis without jaundice and cerebral oedema. Aspirin must stop, dextrose corrects hypoglycaemia, hypertonic saline lowers intracranial pressure, and PICU manages the airway and pressure monitoring. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Liver Failure)

options.text: Commence intravenous aciclovir 500 mg/m2 every 8 hours and intravenous ceftriaxone, perform a lumbar puncture once cerebral imaging is clear, and admit to the ward
options.rationale: There is no fever or meningism, varicella lesions are crusted and CT shows diffuse oedema without focal lesion making encephalitis unlikely; lumbar puncture risks coning with raised intracranial pressure. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Liver Failure)

options.text: Administer activated charcoal 1 g/kg via nasogastric tube, give sodium bicarbonate 1 mmol/kg to alkalinise the urine, and arrange transfer for haemodialysis of salicylate
options.rationale: Charcoal is not indicated 5 days after the last dose; urinary alkalinisation is for acute salicylate poisoning, not for Reye-like mitochondrial injury at a level of 1.8 mmol/L. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Toxinology)

options.text: Withhold all medications, observe in the short stay unit overnight, and arrange a metabolic screen including urine organic acids before deciding further management
options.rationale: Watchful waiting with raised intracranial pressure, hypoglycaemia and INR 2.4 is unsafe; an inherited metabolic disorder is in the differential but does not change the immediate resuscitation priorities. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Liver Failure)

explanation.summary: Reye syndrome is a mitochondrial encephalopathy classically triggered by aspirin during viral illness, particularly varicella and influenza. The picture is hyperammonaemic encephalopathy with hepatic dysfunction, hypoglycaemia and cerebral oedema, but jaundice is characteristically absent. Mortality is high and treatment is supportive: cease the inciting drug, correct glucose, lower intracranial pressure with hypertonic saline and PICU-level care. Inherited fatty acid oxidation disorders such as MCAD deficiency can mimic Reye and should be excluded once stable.
key_points: Hyperammonaemic encephalopathy with raised ALT and AST but normal or near-normal bilirubin is the Reye picture
Aspirin during viral illness, especially varicella and influenza, is the classic trigger in children
Hypoglycaemia and cerebral oedema dominate the early presentation
Treatment is supportive: stop aspirin, give dextrose, hypertonic saline and PICU care
Inherited metabolic disorders such as MCAD deficiency mimic Reye and require post-stabilisation workup
explanation.pearls: Aspirin is contraindicated under 16 years of age in Australia outside Kawasaki disease and specific cardiology indications; any child presenting with encephalopathy after a viral illness needs salicylate, paracetamol and ammonia levels in the first hour.
